summary:
*The Gray Protector. The Exile-Smith. Master of Crafts. Lord of the Invisible Art. The Taskmaster. Father of the Duergar.*

Laduguer is the grim patron of the duergar: a god of labor, craft, invisibility, discipline, enslavement, survival, and remembered wrongs. He is worshiped wherever the hammer is raised without song, wherever the forge is treated as altar and courtroom alike, and wherever a people has learned to make endurance into law.

His faith is not gentle. It does not promise comfort, beauty, reconciliation, or release from grievance. It promises that suffering can be made useful. It promises that exile can become a fortress, that humiliation can become steel, that silence can become concealment, and that a broken people may still build something hard enough to outlast those who cast them down.

To the duergar, Laduguer is father, judge, protector, and taskmaster. He is the one who did not ask them to forgive. He is the one who taught them how to work in darkness until darkness became an advantage. He is the one who looked upon slavery, loss, mutation, estrangement, and shame, and answered not with tears but with tools.

He is not merely a god of craft. Craft under Laduguer is doctrine. A weapon is an argument. A tunnel is a vow. A chain is a policy. A hidden city is a scripture of refusal. A perfect gear, shaped in silence by scarred hands, is holier to him than a thousand hymns sung by the idle.

His cruelty begins where survival becomes sacred beyond question. He teaches discipline until tenderness looks like weakness. He teaches memory until mercy looks like betrayal. He teaches labor until the worker becomes less important than the work. In his darkest temples, the lash and the hammer keep the same rhythm.
tax:
Laduguer is a unique minor deity of the Dwarven Pantheon and the principal divine patron of the duergar. His place is defined by divine office, sustained worship, portfolio authority, and the enduring spiritual identity of the gray dwarves.

His essential classification rests on divinity, duergar patronage, lawful evil alignment, sacred craft, toil, invisibility, exile, discipline, slavery, and vengeance. He is not an ancestral memory, a mortal culture-hero, a forge-spirit, or a powerful underworld dwarf elevated by rumor. He is a god whose will has shaped a people’s understanding of survival, work, obedience, and grievance.

Laduguer’s divinity gathers around hard continuities: the clan that remembers its wound, the forge that never goes cold, the tunnel dug by generations who never saw its end, the slave ledger kept as carefully as a holy text, the invisible scout who returns with the enemy’s gate-plan, the hammer passed from a dead taskmaster to an apprentice who hated him and still learned the craft.

His lawful nature is not abstract reverence for order. It is discipline under pressure: schedules, quotas, ranks, punishments, oaths, craft standards, clan obligations, inheritance of duty, and the binding of each life to the endurance of the whole. His evil is not random malice, but the steady conversion of pain into authority over others.

The name *Exul Faber* suits him well: the Exiled Smith, the maker cast out, the craftsman whose work was sharpened by rejection. It frames his worship as both wound and answer. Laduguer is the god who makes exile productive and then demands that productivity justify everything done in its name.
ap:
**Primary Form.** Laduguer is most often depicted as a stern gray dwarf with a powerful, compact build, broad shoulders, and the heavy proportions of duergar ancestry. His skin is gray, ash-gray, or stone-dark, often marked by forge scars, old burns, or the dust of mines and workshops. His face is broad, hard-lined, and unsmiling, with a square jaw, deep-set eyes, and a fixed, assessing stare.

**Eyes, Hair, and Beard.** His eyes are commonly shown as pale gray, white, dull red, or furnace-orange beneath heavy brows. His beard is usually iron-gray, ash-white, blackened gray, or smoke-stained white, worn in rigid braids, metal clasps, square-cut lengths, or tightly bound rings. Some depictions show him bald above the brow, with a heavy beard and a bare scalp marked by soot, scars, or iron runes.

**Armor and Vestments.** He wears practical armor, smith’s leathers, chain aprons, dark mail, or heavy plate of iron, blackened steel, or dull gray metal. His clothing is severe and workmanlike: thick belts, reinforced gloves, bracers, hobnailed boots, tool harnesses, and aprons marked by heat and hammer work. Sacred images often add an iron crown, a forge-mask raised above the face, a taskmaster’s collar, or a rigid mantle of linked metal plates.

**Carried Implements.** He is commonly shown carrying a warhammer, smith’s hammer, pick, chisel, chain, or heavy shield. In formal depictions, the hammer may be held upright before him, rested across one shoulder, gripped above an anvil, or planted head-down like a badge of office. Chains may hang from his belt, coil around one forearm, or lie across the anvil beside him.

**Iconographic Marks.** His visible symbols include anvils, hammers, broken arrows, shields, chains, square runes, forge tongs, measuring rods, keys, and worked stone blocks. These symbols are usually rendered in iron, gray stone, black metal, or ash-white relief rather than bright ornament.

**Supernatural Depictions.** His body may appear partly veiled by gray dust, forge smoke, or shadow. His footsteps may leave dark prints in stone dust. His hammer may glow at the striking face while the haft remains black. Iron tools near him may appear straighter, heavier, or more sharply defined, and the shadows around his hands may obscure the exact moment of movement.
eco:
**Distribution and Habitat.** Laduguer’s worship is concentrated among duergar communities that preserve traditions of exile, compulsory labor, forge discipline, hidden settlement, and retaliatory endurance. His shrines most often occur in subterranean cities, mine-temples, forge halls, military workshops, slave barracks, armories, clan vaults, punishment chambers, tunnel works, and fortified underroads. His cult is strongest in settlements where survival depends on secrecy, industrial output, hardened craft, and strict command over workers, soldiers, captives, and apprentices.

**Cultic Sustenance.** Laduguer’s divine presence is sustained by disciplined labor, duergar worship, forge rites, clan oaths, vengeance pledges, slave codes, and the sacred preservation of exile-memory. His cult is strengthened by completed works rather than ornamental offerings: weapons forged, tunnels cut, walls raised, chains riveted, gates reinforced, quotas met, enemies repaid, and secrets kept. A finished tool, made under hardship and used for the protection or advancement of the clan, is a more characteristic offering than song, feast, or public display.

**Ritual Transmission.** The faith renews itself through apprenticeship, labor obligation, clan instruction, forge initiation, and the inheritance of grievance. Children are taught tool-care, silence, endurance, and suspicion alongside sacred history. Smiths, miners, engineers, overseers, and shield-wardens may pass through rites marking the first true work completed under hardship, the first weapon accepted by a priest, the first oath of secrecy, the first commanded labor-gang, or the first act of vengeance performed on behalf of clan or city. Initiation is often practical: a task completed without complaint, a flaw corrected under punishment, a hidden passage memorized, or a chain forged to standard.

**Spatial and Social Organization.** Laduguerite cults are hierarchical, industrious, and closely tied to production, defense, and discipline. Their priesthoods commonly include forge-priests, taskmasters, smith-judges, mine-wardens, slave overseers, military engineers, and keepers of ancestral wrongs. Shrines are usually placed where work and authority meet: beside anvils, beneath foundries, inside mine offices, near slave pens, behind armories, or within halls where clan judgments are pronounced. Sacred space is rarely idle. It smelts, measures, audits, punishes, repairs, and records.

**Community Interactions.** Laduguer’s cult functions as a religious-industrial institution within duergar society. It preserves craft standards, blesses tools and fortifications, regulates labor, records clan duty, trains workers and overseers, and teaches resistance to domination through discipline of body and will. To loyal duergar, it may provide continuity, protection, technical mastery, and a sacred explanation for survival after exile. To captives, debtors, dissenters, and subject peoples, it is often an apparatus of forced labor, surveillance, punishment, and inherited resentment. Its authority is most visible where the same priest can bless a hammer, judge a worker, assign a slave, and dedicate a weapon to an ancestral grievance.

**Rivalries and External Pressures.** Laduguer’s worship is most active where duergar communities face scarcity, siege, psychic threat, ancestral enemies, labor shortage, disputed tunnels, or pressure from rival powers. The cult opposes forces that weaken duergar secrecy, undermine craft discipline, liberate bound labor, dissolve clan hierarchy, or soften inherited grievance into reconciliation. It competes with gentler craft traditions, rival dwarven priesthoods, free labor movements, rebellious slaves, undisciplined warbands, and any authority that treats work as separate from obedience. Its presence usually indicates a community organized around survival through production: hard to find, hard to conquer, hard to pity, and harder still to change.
beh:
**Temperament.** Laduguer is severe, exacting, suspicious, and slow to display approval. He is not commonly described as wrathful in a sudden or uncontrolled way; his anger is measured, punitive, and corrective. He treats failure as a flaw to be identified, punished, and either repaired or discarded. He values endurance, obedience, secrecy, craft excellence, and useful suffering more than courage, beauty, mercy, or devotion expressed through words.

**Divine Priorities.** Laduguer favors work that preserves duergar strength: weapons, armor, gates, tunnels, chains, fortifications, hidden roads, siegeworks, mining systems, and tools made to endure generations of use. He approves of labor performed under hardship, craft completed without complaint, secrets kept under pressure, and vengeance prepared patiently rather than spent in undisciplined rage. Waste offends him, especially wasted labor, wasted metal, wasted pain, and wasted opportunity for retaliation.

**Dealings with Worshipers.** Laduguer’s blessings usually take practical forms. He may steady a smith’s hand, reveal a hidden fault in stone, strengthen a wall, conceal a tunnel, harden a worker against exhaustion, or grant invisibility to a scout sent on clan business. His favor often increases the recipient’s burden rather than relieving it. A blessed smith is expected to produce better work; a protected city is expected to become more disciplined; a survivor of hardship is expected to convert survival into duty.

**Standards of Obedience.** Laduguer expects hierarchy to be maintained through labor, punishment, oath, and example. Priests, taskmasters, smiths, mine-wardens, and clan rulers are judged by output, discipline, secrecy, and the durability of what they command. Compassion is tolerated only when it strengthens the community or preserves a useful servant. Mercy that weakens order, reduces production, exposes secrets, or interrupts vengeance is treated as negligence.

**Response to Failure.** Failure is not always unforgivable, but it must be made useful. A flawed blade may be reforged, a defeated soldier reassigned, a careless apprentice scarred into attentiveness, or a failed overseer reduced to labor under those once beneath him. Repeated failure, public weakness, betrayed secrets, broken craft-oaths, and wasteful sentiment invite harsher punishment. Laduguer’s punishments often mirror the failure: a careless mason is crushed by unstable stone, a false smith’s tools turn against him, an oathbreaker becomes visible when concealment is most needed, and a negligent taskmaster is bound to the work he mismanaged.

**Attitude Toward Duergar.** Laduguer is protective of the duergar as a people, but not indulgent toward individual duergar. He preserves the clan, city, forge, and ancestral grievance before the comfort of any single worshiper. His protection is therefore real but conditional. He guards hidden settlements, strengthens workers, and resists forces that would dominate duergar minds or destroy duergar independence, yet he also demands obedience, labor, silence, and continued remembrance of old wrongs.

**Attitude Toward Captives and Slaves.** Laduguer’s faith treats bondage as a tool of order and production. Captives are valued according to labor, knowledge, ransom, punishment, or use in vengeance. A slave who becomes skilled may be preserved; a slave who resists may be broken; a slave who proves strategically useful may be elevated without being freed in any moral sense. This treatment reflects one of the darkest features of his doctrine: suffering is condemned when inflicted upon the duergar by enemies, but sanctified when imposed by the duergar for survival, industry, or repayment.

**Attitude Toward Enemies.** Laduguer favors patient retaliation over theatrical violence. His enemies are undermined through hidden roads, sabotage, invisible scouts, engineered collapse, stolen tools, delayed traps, and weapons prepared long before open conflict begins. He values revenge that strengthens the duergar position, not revenge that merely satisfies anger. An enemy defeated by superior craft, secrecy, and discipline is a more fitting offering than one destroyed in a moment of rage.

**Personality in Worship.** In prayer and omen, Laduguer is terse, practical, and demanding. His signs rarely comfort. A tool aligned beside unfinished work, a hammer ringing without a hand, a crack appearing in flawed metal, or a tunnel revealed at the edge of torchlight may all indicate his attention. His approval is often shown by durability: the gate holds, the weapon does not break, the worker survives, the hidden road remains undiscovered.

**Moral Pattern.** Laduguer’s behavior is governed by the belief that hardship must produce strength, strength must serve the people, and the people must never again be made helpless. His protection becomes oppressive because he does not separate survival from control. He is most dangerous where his doctrine is partly correct: where labor truly preserves life, secrecy truly prevents conquest, and discipline truly resists domination. His evil lies in making those necessities permanent, sacred, and transferable onto others as bondage.
traitsText:
**Tags:** Unique, Deity, Dwarf, Duergar, Lawful, Evil, Extraplanar, Immortal, Divine, Earth, Metal, Forge, Craft, Toil, Invisibility, Mental, Fear, Compulsion, Contract, Oath, Slavery, Vengeance, Exile, Discipline, Tyranny

**Alignment** lawful evil
**Portfolio** duergar, toil, craft, smithing, mining, engineering, invisibility, discipline, slavery, exile, vengeance, harsh survival, hidden strength
**Domains** Artifice, Earth, Evil, Law, Protection
**Subdomains** Caves, Construct, Defense, Metal, Toil, Tyranny, Industry, Slavery, Wards
**Favored Weapon** warhammer
**Symbol** a broken arrow above a shield, a gray hammer over an anvil, or an unseen hand gripping a chain
**Sacred Colors** gray, black, iron, ash-white, and dull red
**Favored Servants** duergar, gray dwarves, forge-priests, smiths, miners, engineers, invisible scouts, taskmasters, slavers, shield-wardens, undercity rulers, oathbound laborers, and those who turn exile into industry

Laduguer’s lawful trait is expressed through clan hierarchy, work codes, forge discipline, military obedience, craft standards, slave law, secrecy oaths, and the inherited obligations of a people who define survival as duty.

His evil lies in the sanctification of hardness. He does not merely permit suffering; he assigns it purpose. Pain becomes training, bondage becomes productivity, vengeance becomes memory, and obedience becomes the price of protection.

Earth belongs to him as worked depth: mine, tunnel, foundation, hidden road, sealed vault, defensive wall, and the silence of stone around labor. Metal belongs to him as the material of transformation: ore into tool, tool into weapon, weapon into law, law into chain.

Invisibility is one of his central signs. It is not only stealth, but the religious answer to erasure. The duergar were unseen in suffering; Laduguer teaches them to become unseen in power.
abilities:
**Invisible Art (Sp)**
Laduguer grants the sacred concealment of the duergar: hidden movement, unseen labor, concealed armies, masked workshops, and works of craft whose makers remain unknown until the result is already irreversible. His invisibility is rarely playful. It is the silence before a hammer falls.

**Forge-Mastery (Su)**
Tools, weapons, armor, chains, locks, engines, and stoneworks made under Laduguer’s blessing become severe, durable, and purposeful. They seldom display unnecessary ornament. Their beauty lies in inevitability: a gate that holds, a hammer that never twists in the hand, a chain that does exactly what its maker intended.

**Anvil of Discipline (Su)**
Laduguer hardens the mind against terror, domination, exhaustion, humiliation, and despair. Those blessed by him may endure what would break others, but the protection often leaves little room for tenderness. A mind hammered flat enough to resist fear may also resist love.

**Labor-Bond (Su)**
Oaths of work, debt, apprenticeship, punishment, and servitude may become supernaturally binding under his witness. Such bonds do not always appear as chains. They may manifest as inability to abandon a task, shame at idleness, tools that grow heavy in disobedient hands, or dreams of the unfinished work calling the oath-breaker back.

**Gray Protector’s Ward (Su)**
Laduguer can shield a forge, clanhold, tunnel, army, or hidden city from discovery and violation. These wards favor silence, stone, metal, disciplined patrols, and concealed mechanisms over radiant barriers. A protected place may not feel blessed. It feels difficult to find, harder to enter, and dangerous to misunderstand.

**Vengeance Tempered (Su)**
He preserves old wrongs in memory, metal, and stone. A weapon forged for vengeance may remember the oath that shaped it. A clan record may resist alteration. A gate may deny the descendants of betrayers. Laduguer’s vengeance is not hot. It is cooled, reheated, folded, and struck until it holds an edge.

**Taskmaster’s Sight (Su)**
Laduguer perceives flaw and inefficiency in work done under oath: weak beams, careless rivets, false measures, wasted labor, hidden idleness, poor discipline, and craft performed without respect for use. His correction may come as insight, shame, injury, or sudden failure before a greater disaster.

**Burden Made Useful (Su)**
Under Laduguer’s influence, suffering can be turned into capacity. Captivity teaches locks. Exile teaches hidden roads. Hunger teaches rationing. Humiliation teaches silence. Pain teaches endurance. This power is terrible because it often works, and because those who benefit from it may begin to impose the same burdens on others.

**Chain-Sanctification (Su)**
Chains, slave marks, work-gangs, and compulsory labor systems may be consecrated in his name. The consecration increases order, output, and obedience, while deepening the spiritual stain upon those who wield bondage as policy. Laduguer’s faith does not treat chains as shameful when the faithful hold the key.

**Stone-Remembered Oath (Su)**
An oath sworn in a Laduguerite forge, mine, vault, or shrine may be remembered by the worked stone itself. Walls may whisper the oath to descendants. Tools may refuse the oath-breaker. Hidden doors may close to those who abandon the duty for which they were opened.

**Hammer of Unseen Judgment (Sp)**
Laduguer may strike through hidden agents, delayed mechanisms, collapsing supports, invisible warriors, or the sudden failure of an enemy’s trusted tool. His wrath often appears as accident until the faithful recognize the craft behind it.
bg:
Laduguer’s history is inseparable from duergar memory.

He is the god of the gray dwarves after abandonment, after enslavement, after psychic violation, after the long work of becoming a people who could survive what had been done to them. His myths do not usually soften this history. They keep it sharp. The wrong was real. The wound was deep. The answer was not forgiveness, but craft, secrecy, discipline, and the refusal to remain weak.

In duergar telling, Laduguer did what gentler powers would not. He did not ask the broken to be noble for the comfort of those who had failed them. He taught them the invisible arts. He taught them to labor without witness, strike without warning, hide what must live, and remember what must one day be repaid. He took a people treated as victims, slaves, and shameful kin, and gave them a theology in which endurance itself became proof of worth.

That theology is powerful because it contains truth. Work can preserve life. Discipline can defeat domination. Secrecy can protect the hunted. Craft can restore agency to hands once forced into servitude. A people that cannot afford softness may survive by becoming hard.

Laduguer’s darkness lies in his refusal to stop there.

The faith that begins as survival becomes domination. The memory of slavery becomes permission to enslave. The discipline that resisted torment becomes the discipline inflicted on children, captives, workers, and dissenters. The secrecy that protected the city becomes the secrecy that hides cruelty. The hammer that rebuilt the people becomes the hammer under which the people are kept bent.

Deep Duerra is often remembered beside him as the queen of invisible art, conquest of the mind, and duergar expansion beyond mere endurance. Their cults may cooperate, rival one another, or divide authority by city, clan, and doctrine. Laduguer is the older iron of duergar survival: the law of work, craft, punishment, and ancestral grievance. Where his influence is strongest, even conquest must justify itself as labor owed to the past.

His estrangement from the broader dwarven divine family remains a sacred wound. Laduguer’s worshipers do not usually treat that estrangement as a tragedy to be healed. They treat it as evidence. The exile-god stands apart because his people stand apart; his people stand apart because standing apart kept them alive. Reconciliation, in such temples, is often suspected as another name for surrender.

Laduguer is defeated not by despising work, mocking duergar bitterness, or offering easy forgiveness to those whose history was carved by real suffering. He is defeated by a harder and rarer thing: labor that honors the worker, memory that does not require inherited cruelty, discipline that protects without enslaving, and survival that finally asks what life is for beyond not being destroyed.
note:
Use Laduguer where survival has curdled into doctrine.

He belongs in stories about duergar cities, hidden forges, slave economies, ancestral grievance, invisible war, inherited exile, craft-pride, and communities that truly were wronged but have built their answer from chains. He is strongest when his faithful are not foolish or merely cruel. They should often be competent, disciplined, technically brilliant, and able to point to real dangers that their harshness has held at bay.

A Laduguerite settlement may be safer than its neighbors, better armed, harder to infiltrate, and more capable of withstanding siege or domination. Its bridges may not fall. Its gates may not open to enemies. Its soldiers may not break. Its tools may last for generations. Its children may never be allowed to forget why this is so.

The cost should be visible in small things before it becomes monstrous: a worker hiding an injured hand because rest will shame the family; a child praised for silence rather than kindness; a captured enemy assigned a tool number before a name is asked; a priest describing mercy as inefficient; a masterpiece locked away because beauty that serves no function is considered suspect.

Laduguer’s servants can be antagonists, patrons, uneasy allies, or tragic mirrors. They may provide invisible passage, impossible engineering, siegecraft, weapons, labor, or knowledge of deep roads. Their help should feel reliable and heavy. They solve problems by making them into work, and then by deciding who must be made to perform it.

The central question Laduguer poses is not, “Will you endure hardship?”

It is, “When hardship has made you strong, who will you put beneath the hammer next?”
